body_txt: Whether something between two (or more) persons happened " by mutual consent " is often the dividing line between an acceptable and an unacceptable act. However, human emotions and relationships are complex and multifaceted, especially during moments of confusion or stress. Even when a person verbally gives assent to someone else's request or demand, various issues — a significant status difference between the parties, the age, mental/emotional condition of the one giving consent, and other potential conflicts of interest — may call the freedom of that consent into question. Imagine that Bob is so poor he can barely afford to feed his children. The wealthy Alice approaches him and offers a significant sum of money for something that Bob really doesn't want to give up, like a kidney, or unprotected sexual intercourse with no parental rights over any resulting child. Bob may accept Alice's offer, and it may appear that he did so willingly, but was he really in any position to refuse? The Sliding Scale of Consent Versus Exploitation doesn't have to be about money. It can be about social status, intimacy, drugs, or any kind of MacGuffin . It can also be a discussion about whether or not a certain character is able to consent to a certain thing in the first place. The character might be too young or inexperienced , drunk or drugged, intellectually challenged, unconscious , or similar. It might also have to do with the nature of the relationship : a boss and an employee , including Hanky-Panky with the Help , a health care worker and a patient , a parent and child (even if said child is fully grown), a teacher and student , a clergyperson and a member of their congregation , a god/goddess and a human (or other non-divine entity) , or something along those lines. More romanticized depictions of prostitution usually gloss over this too, ignoring issues such as poverty, addiction or threats from pimps that can keep people in the industry. Even apart from that, age, desperation or power differential can be an issue, as with sex between a master and slave . The trope may also apply in cases where one party is, if not immortal, at least Really Seven Hundred Years Old , and the other is a regular human being. The related general concept is Hobson's Choice , when a person is offered a "free choice" that has only one real option. Compare Leonine Contract . Appeal to Force may be used; for extra No Yay , the even more extreme variant An Offer You Can't Refuse might get used instead. Adaptational Consent often overlaps, either clarifying one way or another whether a situation was truly consensual or doing the opposite and muddying a clear-cut situation into being Questionable. See also the Useful Notes page on Consent . This trope is not to be confused with the webcomic that's only one letter away, Questionable Content . If it comes up in a conversation about whether, say, a teenager could truly consent to a relationship with another teenager who's really a 200-year-old vampire , it might bring up Fantastic Legal Weirdness . No Real Life Examples, Please!
